title: Hybrid Generic Pipe Dreams for Lower-upper Varieties
speaker: Allen Knutson
abstract:
There has been a lot of recent work connecting the two pipe dream formulae for Schubert polynomials (classic and bumpless). One strand is the hybrid pipe dreams of Udell and myself, giving 2n
 different pipe dream formulae; an orthogonal one is the generic pipe dreams of Zinn-Justin and myself, which compute the equivariant cohomology classes of "lower-upper varieties" (whose definition I will recall), and recover the Schubert polynomials as leading terms. In this talk I'll unify these strands, giving 2n
tags: Workshop on Combinatorics of Enumerative Geometry

proof_stages.label: Classical and bumpless Schubert formulas
proof_stages.summary: The talk sets up classical pipe dreams and bumpless pipe dreams, defines their weighted polynomials, and recalls that both compute the same double Schubert polynomial despite having very different terms.

proof_stages.label: Row-hybrid pipe dream interpolation
proof_stages.summary: A hybridization chooses each row to be classical/leftward or bumpless/rightward, defines the corresponding hybrid pipe dream polynomial, and states that all such hybridizations still give the double Schubert polynomial.

proof_stages.label: Generic pipe dream polynomial
proof_stages.summary: Generic pipe dreams allow all tile types and pipe recrossings, with weights involving a,b,x,y, and their leading b- and a-forms recover respectively the classical and bumpless formulas.

proof_stages.label: Hybrid generic pipe dreams
proof_stages.summary: The two interpolation directions are combined by allowing generic leftward and rightward rows, producing hybrid generic pipe dream polynomials claimed to be independent of the chosen hybridization.

proof_stages.label: Yang-Baxter moves and recurrence
proof_stages.summary: New diamond-shaped auxiliary tiles and Yang-Baxter-type local identities move a catalyst through adjacent rows to prove hybridization independence, while a same-direction version gives a divided-difference-style recurrence in the permutation.

proof_stages.label: Lower-upper geometry and equivariant classes
proof_stages.summary: The lower-upper scheme of pairs (X,Y) with XY lower triangular and YX upper triangular is recalled as a reduced complete intersection with permutation-indexed components, and the generic pipe dream polynomial is identified with equivariant cohomology and CSM-type classes attached to these varieties.

proof_stages.label: Gröbner degeneration and flux origin of tiles
proof_stages.summary: Suitable lex/revlex degenerations of matrix Schubert and lower-upper equations produce flux equalities whose components are encoded by drawing the generic pipe dream tiles.

examples.note: A drawn n=4 pipe dream with colored pipes is used to make the local tiles, connectivity condition, and crossing rule concrete.

examples.name: Two triangulations of a square
examples.note: The square triangulation analogy illustrates why two positive formulas can compute the same invariant without giving a canonical term-by-term bijection.
examples.engagement_reason: he uses the two square triangulations only as an analogy for noncanonical bijections, without computing or constructing anything on them

passes.reason: the regular square with its two diagonal triangulations is symmetric and is used to show that no canonical symmetric matching of simplices exists

examples.name: Permutation 132 comparison
examples.note: For pi=132, the speaker computes the two classical terms and the two bumpless terms to show concretely that the formulas agree but do not match weights termwise.
examples.engagement_reason: he computes the classical and bumpless pipe-dream terms for 132 and compares their weights explicitly

passes.reason: 132 is a length-one simple-transposition case used to show the naive x-y weight-preserving bijection between the two formulas fails
The permutation 132 is a one-inversion minimal nontrivial case used to show no x,y-weight-preserving bijection exists.
The permutation 132 is a length-one simple transposition and is used to show that the classical and bumpless formulas are not naively weight-bijective.

examples.name: Generic pipe dreams for pi=12
examples.note: Two n=2 generic pipe dreams, one classical-looking and one rotated bumpless-looking, illustrate the relaxed recrossing/all-tiles definition before larger nonclassical cases are mentioned.
examples.engagement_reason: he draws concrete generic pipe dreams for pi=12, checks the boundary connections, and identifies them as classical or rotated bumpless
